source_signals.title: Tech oligarchs reshape humanity while billionaires of old seem quaint
source_signals.summary: From Gates to Musk and Altman, today’s ultra-rich steer AI and tech, raising questions about who decides the future When Bill Gates became the first modern IT mogul to reach the apex of wealth and power in 1992, the world was a very different place. Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year. The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Dr TikTok: patients diagnose chronic illnesses with anonymous commenters’ help
source_signals.summary: TikTok users increasingly say the app has steered them toward diagnosing medical problems not yet identified Malina Lee, a 31-year-old wedding baker based in San Antonio, Texas, joined TikTok during the Covid pandemic lockdowns in 2020. Like many people at the time, she was bored and began using the platform to pass the time and advertise her business. She didn’t expect a cancer diagnosis. Four years after Lee joined the app, a commenter with the username “PickleFart” told her that her neck looked asymmetrical in a way that could suggest she had a goiter – an enlarged thyroid gland – and that she should get it checked out. The anonymous amateur clinician turned out to be right – Lee had thyroid cancer, received treatment quickly, and, less than a year later, was cancer free. Continue reading...

The law, which was a carve-out of the European Union’s ePrivacy Directive, was put in place in 2021 as a temporary measure allowing companies to use automated detection technologies to scan messages for harms, including child sexual abuse material (CSAM), grooming and sextortion . However, it expired on 3 April, and the EU parliament decided not to vote to extend it, amid privacy concerns from some lawmakers. source_signals.title: Super Mario what?! The seven best obscure Mario games
source_signals.summary: As The Super Mario Galaxy Movie storms the box office, we look back at the best forgotten games inspired by Tetris, Lemmings and … vitamins? It should be no surprise that the latest Super Mario movie is smashing box office records – despite the, let’s say mixed, reviews . Nintendo’s iconic plumber has been a pop culture staple for 45 years, starring in some of the bestselling video games ever made, from the original Donkey Kong through to the joyous Super Mario Bros Wonder and the chaotic Mario Kart World. But as with any storied showbiz career, there have been some lesser works. Who can forget – or actually remember – Hotel Mario, a door-shutting puzzle game for the doomed Philips CD-i console? Or what about Mario Teaches Typing, a 1992 educational game for the PC in which players navigate the Mushroom Kingdom by … correctly inputting words. Yet there have also been genuine treasures lost along the way. Here, then, are seven of our favourite much-overlooked Mario odysseys. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: Cardiff stages thriller while Women’s Six Nations favourites show strength
source_signals.summary: Wales and Scotland produced drama at the Principality Stadium while England and Ireland drew a record crowd as the tournament began at major venues Sporting theatre reached Shakespearean heights at the Principality Stadium on Saturday as Wales and Scotland produced the best match of the opening Women’s Six Nations weekend. Welsh hearts broke seven minutes past the full 80 as Scotland managed to get a comeback win over the line. The fixture had sensational tries and late drama, and played out on BBC Two. The only thing missing was the type of crowd that such a thriller deserved. Wales hosted the game at the national stadium, the only one taking place there during this year’s tournament, watched by 10,569 supporters. The number is a record for a fixture between the two teams in Wales but if it had been held next door at Cardiff Arms Park, which has an official capacity of just over 12,000, or at Cardiff City Stadium, which holds about 33,000 and is hosting Wales men v Fiji in July, the atmosphere would have translated better to the players as well as to those watching at home. Continue reading...

source_signals.title: History beckons for I Am Maximus as Red Rum’s record comes in to view | Greg Wood
source_signals.summary: The two-time Grand National winner is surely second only to ‘Rummy’ and will be a strong favourite at Aintree in 2027 Relentless drama, no significant injuries to any of the 34 runners and a winner who inked himself on to the exclusive list of Aintree legends with the possibility of better to come next year. Saturday’s Grand National produced everything racing could realistically hope for and more, and if I Am Maximus can stay sound and return to Aintree in 2027 for a fourth run in the race, it promises to be one of the highlights of the sporting year. There was a 45-year wait for the next dual Grand National winner after Red Rum’s second success in 1974. Now, seven years after Tiger Roll’s second victory, I Am Maximus is only the third horse since the mid 1930s to register win number two, and the first since the peerless “Rummy” to win in nonconsecutive years. Having also finished a close second in 2025, he is arguably already second only to Red Rum in the list of all-time Aintree greats. He is a marketing person’s dream: a sporting hero who carries a gladiatorial name into combat, always rises to the challenge in the most famous race of the year, and won’t overturn his car or pick a fight in a nightclub. lead_story.title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
lead_story.summary: Péter Magyar has defeated Viktor Orbán in a historic Hungarian election landslide, ending Orbán’s 16-year rule and opening a new phase for a country long at the centre of Europe’s political and security anxieties. The result will reverberate in Brussels and Moscow alike, and tests whether a formerly dominant nationalist machine can be displaced at the ballot box.
lead_story.source_signal_title: Orbán era swept away by Péter Magyar's Hungary election landslide
body: Péter Magyar has ended Viktor Orbán’s long dominance of Hungarian politics, in a result that marks the most significant change of government in Budapest in 16 years. The 45-year-old former insider persuaded a majority of voters to turn away from a leader who had built one of Europe’s most durable nationalist machines.

The scale of the defeat matters well beyond Hungary. Orbán has been a regular irritant in Brussels and one of the European Union’s most awkward members on issues from rule of law to sanctions on Russia. His removal will be read in European capitals as a political correction, while in Moscow it is likely to be viewed with far less enthusiasm.

For many Hungarians, the vote appears to have been driven by frustration with the pace of change at home and by exhaustion with a politics dominated by one figure for so long. Magyar’s rise also suggests that even entrenched incumbents can be displaced if an opposition figure can unite discontent behind a credible alternative.

The result is still likely to raise questions about what kind of government Magyar will try to build, and how quickly he can turn an anti-Orbán mandate into a workable programme. He inherits a country whose domestic politics, media landscape and institutions have all been shaped by Orbán’s rule since 2010.

Hungary’s position will also matter for Europe’s security debate, not least because the country sits at the junction of several current anxieties: Russia’s war on Ukraine, energy supply and the EU’s own internal cohesion. A change of leadership will not resolve those tensions on its own, but it does alter the balance in a bloc that has struggled to contain Orbán’s veto power.

For now, the scale of the landslide is the story. A political order that once looked entrenched has been broken at the ballot box, and the consequences will be felt in Budapest, Brussels and Moscow alike.

stories.title: Trump's threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
stories.summary: Donald Trump’s escalation against Iran is turning the conflict into a test of endurance between Iran’s capacity to absorb strikes and Trump’s tolerance for the war’s costs. The strategic dilemma remains unchanged, but the danger of wider regional spillover is growing.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump's threat raises risks and leaves predicaments unchanged
body: Donald Trump’s escalation against Iran has turned the conflict into a test of endurance, but it has not changed the basic strategic dilemma. Iran is trying to show it can absorb punishment and keep operating; Trump is trying to prove he can impose costs without being drawn into a wider war.

That is why the latest threats are more dangerous than decisive. They increase the risk of miscalculation, especially around the Strait of Hormuz and other choke points that matter to shipping, oil and allied military deployments. Yet they do little to solve the underlying problem of how either side can force the other to bend.

The conflict now carries a built-in imbalance: each new strike or warning can be presented as resolve, but each also raises the chance of spillover into the wider region. Gulf states, energy markets and Western governments all have reason to fear that a confrontation framed as controlled pressure could drift into something larger and harder to contain.

What happens next depends less on rhetoric than on whether both sides see an exit. If Iran digs in and Trump decides that escalation is politically affordable, the conflict could settle into a damaging war of attrition with no clear off-ramp. If either side blinks, it may do so only after the costs have already risen.

stories.title: Trump attacks Pope over criticism of Iran war
stories.summary: Donald Trump has attacked Pope Leo XIV after the pontiff criticised the Iran war, accusing him of being weak on crime and terrible for foreign policy. The exchange shows how the conflict is spilling into wider arguments about morality and power.
stories.source_signal_title: Trump attacks Pope over criticism of Iran war
body: Donald Trump has attacked Pope Leo XIV after the pontiff criticised the Iran war, accusing the head of the Catholic Church of being weak on crime and terrible for foreign policy. The exchange shows how the conflict is spilling beyond the battlefield and into arguments about morality, authority and the limits of power.

The Pope’s intervention matters because it comes from one of the few global figures whose influence is moral rather than military. By responding in such combative terms, Trump has turned a religious criticism into another front in the political fight over his handling of Iran.

The row is also a reminder that the war is already reshaping public debate far beyond Washington and Tehran. As casualties, sanctions and energy shocks deepen, leaders are being pressed to justify not only strategy but values. That leaves little room for restraint in the rhetoric around the conflict, even when restraint might help lower the temperature.

stories.title: Pope prioritises world's fastest-growing Catholic region in major Africa tour
stories.summary: Pope Leo XIV is heading to Africa, where the Church sees the continent as central to its future and to its growth across a region that remains strategically important for global Catholicism.
stories.source_signal_title: Pope prioritises world's fastest-growing Catholic region in major Africa tour
body: Pope Leo XIV is heading to Africa as part of a major tour aimed at drawing attention to the continent’s importance to the Catholic Church’s future. The visit reflects a long-running shift in global Catholicism: the Church is growing fastest in parts of Africa, even as its traditional centres in Europe continue to weaken.

For the Vatican, the trip is about more than symbolism. Africa has become central to the Church’s demographic future, its missionary priorities and some of its most difficult political questions, from conflict and poverty to the relationship between religion and state.

The tour will also be watched as a test of how Leo wants to define his papacy internationally. By foregrounding Africa, he is signalling that the Church’s future will not be written only in Rome or the West, but in regions where the Catholic population is expanding most quickly.

The practical challenge is that Africa is not one Catholic story but many. Different countries face different pressures, and the Pope’s message will need to balance celebration of growth with attention to the insecurity and inequality that still shape daily life for many believers.

stories.title: Political turmoil in Indian border state as nine million lose voting rights
stories.summary: Nine million voters have been dropped from the rolls in West Bengal, intensifying fears of exclusion and unfairness in one of India’s most politically sensitive states.
stories.source_signal_title: Political turmoil in Indian border state as nine million lose voting rights
body: West Bengal, one of India’s most politically sensitive states, has been thrown into turmoil after nine million voters were dropped from the electoral rolls. The removals have intensified accusations of exclusion and unfairness in a state where voting rights are already closely tied to wider battles over identity and power.

The immediate concern is that people who believed they were eligible to vote may now find themselves shut out of the electoral process. In a region bordering Bangladesh, questions over documentation, residence and citizenship tend to carry unusually high political weight, making the dispute especially combustible.

The episode is likely to deepen mistrust between parties and institutions at a moment when Indian politics is already marked by fierce arguments over who belongs and who gets to participate. For opposition figures, the scale of the deletions will be presented as evidence of manipulation; for the authorities, the challenge will be to explain the criteria and process clearly enough to restore confidence.

What happens next will depend on whether there is a credible mechanism to challenge the exclusions and reinstate eligible voters. Without that, the dispute risks becoming another long-running grievance in a state where elections are often contested as much over legitimacy as over policy.

stories.title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
stories.summary: The Nigerian Air Force has not addressed reports that a strike on a village market in north-east Nigeria killed civilians while targeting militants, raising fresh concerns about the conduct of the campaign.
stories.source_signal_title: Civilians feared killed after reports of air strike on Nigerian market
body: Reports that the Nigerian Air Force struck a village market in north-east Nigeria while targeting militants have raised fears that civilians were killed in the attack. The air force has not publicly addressed the allegations, leaving questions over what was hit and how the operation was carried out.

The strike matters because it goes to the heart of a long-running and deeply damaging security campaign in the region. Nigeria’s military has repeatedly faced criticism over civilian casualties in operations against insurgents, and each such incident risks eroding trust among people already living amid violence and displacement.

If the reports are confirmed, the political cost could be substantial. For communities in the north-east, the issue is not only the presence of militants but the danger of being caught between insurgent violence and state force. That makes transparency after strikes as important as the military objective itself.

The key uncertainty is whether this was a misfire, a mistaken target or something worse. Until the authorities explain what happened, the incident will fuel concern that the war against militancy is still being fought with too little regard for civilian safety.

stories.title: As Carney eyes Liberal majority, there are risks for him and Poilievre
stories.summary: Mark Carney is trying to turn Monday’s by-elections into a stronger mandate for the Canadian Liberals, but his broad coalition may yet prove difficult to hold together.
stories.source_signal_title: As Carney eyes Liberal majority, there are risks for him and Poilievre
body: Mark Carney is looking to use Monday’s by-elections to strengthen the Liberal Party’s hold on power, but the result could cut both ways. A stronger mandate would help consolidate his authority; a weaker showing would sharpen questions about whether his broad coalition can hold together.

Carney’s appeal has rested on breadth as much as leadership, bringing together voters and factions that do not always share the same priorities. That can be an asset in government, but it can also make discipline harder and policy choices more contested, especially if the party is asked to absorb too many competing interests.

For Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives, the by-elections matter too. A good Liberal result would make it harder to argue that the government is vulnerable, while a poor one could revive the opposition’s claim that Carney’s support is fragile. Either way, the vote will be read as an early measure of political momentum rather than a final verdict.

The stakes are not only parliamentary arithmetic. In a polarised political climate, even local results can reshape perceptions of who is in command, and whether the electorate is rewarding caution, competence or change.

stories.title: Oil back above $100 as US to blockade Iranian ports after peace talks fail
stories.summary: Oil has moved back above $100 a barrel as the US prepares to blockade Iranian ports after weekend peace talks failed, deepening fears of a broader energy crisis and further inflation.
stories.source_signal_title: Oil back above $100 as US to blockade Iranian ports after peace talks fail
body: Oil has climbed back above $100 a barrel as the United States prepares to blockade Iranian ports after peace talks collapsed at the weekend. The move points to a deepening energy shock, with traders now pricing in the possibility that the conflict around Iran will last longer and disrupt supplies further.

The market reaction reflects a simple fear: if shipping through and around the Gulf is obstructed, the consequences will reach well beyond crude prices. Higher oil costs feed into transport, food and manufacturing, raising the risk that an already fraught geopolitical crisis becomes a broader inflation problem.

The blockade threat is also a reminder of how closely diplomacy, military pressure and market behaviour are now linked. Once negotiations fail, the first signs are often seen not in official statements but in shipping routes, insurance premiums and the price of fuel.

What happens next will depend on whether the US follows through and how Iran responds. But even the threat has already changed the mood in energy markets, where the assumption is now that disruption may be measured in weeks, not days.

stories.title: UK could adopt EU single market rules under new legislation
stories.summary: Keir Starmer is preparing legislation that would allow the UK government to adopt EU single market rules without Parliament voting on each step, a significant constitutional and economic shift.
stories.source_signal_title: UK could adopt EU single market rules under new legislation
body: Keir Starmer is preparing legislation that would allow the UK government to adopt EU single market rules without returning to Parliament for a fresh vote each time. That would be a significant constitutional and economic shift, giving ministers a faster route to alignment with the EU in selected areas.

The proposal matters because it touches both trade and sovereignty, the two questions that have defined British politics since Brexit. For businesses, closer rule alignment could reduce friction and make cross-border trade easier. For critics, it would raise concern that major changes could be made with less parliamentary scrutiny than before.

The plan suggests a more pragmatic approach to the UK’s relationship with Brussels, one focused less on symbolic distance and more on practical access. It also reflects the reality that sectors from manufacturing to services remain sensitive to regulatory divergence.

The politics are likely to be difficult. Any move that looks like quiet re-entry into EU rule-making, even in limited form, will provoke debate over mandate and accountability. But the government appears to think the economic gains may outweigh the constitutional discomfort.

stories.title: Rolls-Royce secures nearly £600m in UK government cash to develop small reactors
stories.summary: Rolls-Royce has secured up to £599m from Britain’s national wealth fund to accelerate small modular reactors at Wylfa on Anglesey, a key test of the UK’s nuclear industrial strategy.
stories.source_signal_title: Rolls-Royce secures nearly £600m in UK government cash to develop small reactors
body: Rolls-Royce has secured up to £599m from Britain’s national wealth fund to help develop small modular reactors at Wylfa on Anglesey. The deal is a significant moment for the company’s nuclear ambitions and for the government’s wider attempt to revive a domestic nuclear supply chain.

Small modular reactors are being presented as a way to build nuclear power more quickly and flexibly than with traditional large-scale plants. Supporters say they could provide cleaner, more reliable power and create industrial jobs; sceptics note that the technology is still unproven at scale and faces technical, financial and regulatory hurdles.

Wylfa has long symbolised Britain’s stop-start approach to nuclear development. Backing the site again suggests ministers are willing to bet that public capital can help push the project beyond the planning stage. If it works, it could strengthen the case for a new generation of British nuclear manufacturing.

But the sums involved also underline the risks. Public money can accelerate development, yet it cannot remove the possibility of delay or cost overruns. The success of this investment will depend on whether Rolls-Royce can convert political support into deployable reactors on time and at a price the state can live with.

stories.title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
stories.summary: As the US threatens action in the Strait of Hormuz, the practical and legal meaning of a naval blockade matters for shipping, energy prices and allied military commitments.
stories.source_signal_title: What is a naval blockade and how would it work in Strait of Hormuz?
body: Donald Trump’s threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz has put a rarely used and legally contentious weapon back into the centre of debate. In simple terms, a naval blockade is an attempt to stop ships entering or leaving a port or coastline, usually enforced by military power and backed by international law only in limited circumstances.

The Strait of Hormuz matters because it is one of the world’s most important shipping lanes, carrying a large share of global oil and gas traffic. Any obstruction there would quickly affect energy prices, freight costs and the willingness of allied navies to commit assets to the region.

In practice, a blockade would not be a single event but a sustained operation requiring ships, surveillance, rules of engagement and political backing. It could also raise immediate questions about whether it is lawful, whether it is enforceable and how other states would respond if their commercial vessels were affected.

That is why the issue matters far beyond the Gulf. Even the threat of a blockade can unsettle markets and diplomatic partners, because it signals that the crisis is no longer being managed only through sanctions and strikes, but through control of sea lanes.

stories.title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
stories.summary: US inflation has reached 3.3% as higher pump prices linked to the Iran war feed through to the wider economy, increasing pressure on consumers and policymakers.
stories.source_signal_title: Soaring pump prices drive US inflation to highest level in almost two years
body: US inflation has risen to 3.3%, the highest level in almost two years, as soaring pump prices linked to the Iran war feed through the economy. The latest figures underline how a foreign conflict is now showing up directly in American household costs.

The immediate pressure is on drivers, but the effects do not stop there. Higher fuel prices tend to work through transport costs and supply chains, making it harder for policymakers to judge whether the inflation surge is temporary or the start of something more persistent.

For the White House and the Federal Reserve, the challenge is that the shock is both geopolitical and domestic. Officials can do little to change the oil market quickly, but they will be judged on whether they can prevent a supply shock from becoming a broader inflation problem.

The numbers also raise political risks. As with previous oil shocks, voters often feel the pain first at the pump and only later in the rest of the economy. That can quickly turn a distant war into a kitchen-table issue.

stories.title: Interest in EVs surges in Europe as fuel prices jump after Iran war
stories.summary: Car buyers in the UK, Germany, France and Spain are showing a sharp rise in interest in electric vehicles as petrol costs jump and the conflict in Iran changes consumer behaviour.
stories.source_signal_title: Interest in EVs surges in Europe as fuel prices jump after Iran war
body: Interest in electric vehicles has risen sharply across the UK, Germany, France and Spain as fuel prices jump after the war in Iran. The change suggests that geopolitical shocks are already influencing consumer behaviour, not just government policy and financial markets.

For car buyers, higher petrol costs make the economics of switching to an EV more attractive. Online marketplaces say enquiries have surged, and some analysts believe the shift in demand could become a higher normal rather than a temporary spike if fuel prices stay elevated.

That matters for Europe’s car market and for the broader transition away from fossil fuels. A crisis driven by insecurity in the Middle East may end up nudging consumers towards cleaner transport, even if only because charging looks cheaper than filling up.

The uncertainty is whether the trend will last. If fuel prices retreat, some of the new interest may fade. But even a short-lived shock can change expectations, and car dealers may now have to reckon with a more price-sensitive customer base than before.

stories.title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
stories.summary: The failure of US-Iran talks has put oil and gas markets on alert, with tankers still stranded in the Gulf and analysts warning of further damage to growth and prices.
stories.source_signal_title: Collapse of US-Iran talks heightens fears of prolonged energy shock
body: The failure of US-Iran talks has put oil and gas markets on alert for a prolonged energy shock. With tankers still stranded in the Gulf, traders are now bracing for a period of higher prices, tighter supply and more expensive borrowing for energy-heavy businesses.

The collapse matters because diplomacy had offered at least the possibility of a managed outcome. Instead, the market is left with a more open-ended confrontation in which neither side appears ready to make the concessions the other demands.

That raises the chance that the conflict will be felt in ways that go well beyond the Gulf. If energy prices rise for long enough, the damage can spread to inflation, consumer demand and growth, especially in economies already under pressure.

For now, the picture is one of uncertainty rather than immediate breakdown. But as long as tankers remain stranded and talks are stalled, the assumption in markets is that the shock is not over — and may yet deepen.

stories.title: Private firms providing services to NHS made £1.6bn profit in two years, research finds
stories.summary: Research on £12bn of NHS contracts says private providers made £1.6bn in profit over two years, renewing pressure on ministers to curb profiteering in health outsourcing.
stories.source_signal_title: Private firms providing services to NHS made £1.6bn profit in two years, research finds
body: Private companies providing services to the NHS made £1.6bn in profit over two years on contracts worth £12bn, according to research that is likely to intensify pressure on ministers over outsourcing. The findings have revived long-running arguments about how much public money should flow to firms delivering healthcare and related services.

The report also says a significant share of contracts went to firms with overseas ownership, including businesses linked to tax havens. That will sharpen concerns among critics that the health service is not getting full value for money, particularly when hospitals and patients are already under strain.

The political issue is not simply whether private providers should be involved, but under what terms. Supporters of outsourcing argue it can add capacity and expertise; opponents say the profit motive can distort priorities and drain resources away from care.

The government will now face renewed pressure to set clearer limits on returns and tougher rules on transparency. The challenge is to answer the profit question without disrupting services that still rely on outside providers.

stories.title: Tice £91,000 tax row is 'minor administrative error', party claims
stories.summary: Reform UK says a property company linked to Richard Tice’s finances failed to pay £91,000 in tax on dividends, after reporting by the Sunday Times.
stories.source_signal_title: Tice £91,000 tax row is 'minor administrative error', party claims
body: Reform UK has described a £91,000 tax issue linked to Richard Tice’s property company as a minor administrative error, after reporting in the Sunday Times. The row is awkward for the party’s deputy leader, who has built part of his political appeal around discipline in public life and criticism of the tax system.

The case matters less for the sum itself than for the contrast between rhetoric and conduct. In politics, tax disputes are rarely just about compliance; they also become tests of credibility, particularly for parties that present themselves as anti-establishment or financially prudent.

Reform will want to contain the issue quickly and frame it as an administrative matter rather than a political one. But any suggestion that a senior figure’s business affairs were mishandled will be seized on by opponents as evidence that the party is not immune from the same standards it demands of others.

What happens next will depend on whether the explanation satisfies scrutiny and whether any further details emerge. Until then, the episode is likely to remain a distraction rather than a decisive blow, but one that does little to help the party’s image.

stories.title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
stories.summary: Labour MP Nic Dakin says British Steel needs nationalising by the summer as ownership talks continue, keeping the future of the company and its jobs in focus.
stories.source_signal_title: British Steel 'needs nationalising by the summer'
body: British Steel needs nationalising by the summer, according to Labour MP Nic Dakin, as talks over the company’s ownership continue. The warning reflects the pressure surrounding a business whose future has implications not just for industrial policy, but for jobs and supply chains in steelmaking communities.

British Steel has long symbolised the difficulties Britain faces in keeping heavy industry viable in a high-cost, globally competitive market. Ownership uncertainty makes that harder, because investment decisions become more difficult when the long-term structure of the company remains unresolved.

The case for nationalisation is being framed as the best way to secure stability. Supporters of that view argue that the state may need to step in if private ownership cannot provide the certainty the industry needs. Critics will worry about cost and precedent.

The timing matters because delay tends to narrow options. If talks drag on too long, the government may find itself choosing between a hurried intervention and an even messier collapse in confidence. For workers and local economies, the uncertainty itself is already part of the damage.

stories.title: Starmer says UK will not join Trump's blockade of Iran's ports
stories.summary: Keir Starmer says the UK will not join Donald Trump’s blockade of Iran’s ports, while keeping British minesweepers and anti-drone capabilities in the region.
stories.source_signal_title: Starmer says UK will not join Trump's blockade of Iran's ports
body: Keir Starmer has said the UK will not join Donald Trump’s blockade of Iran’s ports, drawing a line between British and American policy as pressure builds around the Strait of Hormuz. The UK will, however, keep its minesweepers and anti-drone capabilities in the region.

The decision matters because Britain remains one of the allies most closely tied to US military strategy, yet it is clearly trying to avoid being pulled into an escalation it does not control. By refusing to join the blockade, Starmer is signalling caution even as London continues to support maritime security.

The UK’s continuing presence in the region also shows the limits of disengagement. Minesweepers and anti-drone assets are not symbolic; they are part of the effort to keep shipping routes open and protect allied interests if the crisis worsens.

The next question is how long Britain can stay in this middle position. If the blockade threat becomes a prolonged confrontation, the pressure on London to choose between allied solidarity and restraint will only grow.

stories.title: PM to scrap spy chiefs' Hillsborough Law veto
stories.summary: The prime minister is preparing to remove the veto that would have allowed spy chiefs to block intelligence evidence in future public inquiries.
stories.source_signal_title: PM to scrap spy chiefs' Hillsborough Law veto
body: The prime minister is preparing to remove a veto that would have allowed security chiefs to block intelligence evidence in future public inquiries. The move is a significant adjustment to the proposed Hillsborough Law, which is intended to improve transparency and accountability after state failures.

The issue matters because public inquiries exist precisely to establish facts that governments or institutions may prefer not to disclose. Allowing intelligence agencies to veto evidence would have risked undermining public confidence in any process meant to get to the truth.

By scrapping the veto, ministers are signalling that security concerns should not automatically override the public interest in disclosure. That is likely to be welcomed by campaigners who have long argued that the state should not be able to shield itself from scrutiny too easily.

There may still be friction with security officials, who will want reassurance that sensitive material can be protected where necessary. But the direction of travel is clear: the government wants future inquiries to be harder for the state to frustrate.

stories.title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
stories.summary: The government plans to bar deep-fried food from school dinners and push schools toward more vegetables and whole grains, in a major diet overhaul.
stories.source_signal_title: Deep-fried food banned in new plans for school dinners
body: The government is planning to ban deep-fried food from school dinners and push schools towards more vegetables and whole grains. The overhaul is part of a wider attempt to improve children’s diets and tackle obesity through the school meal system.

The move matters because school food is one of the few places where the state can shape habits at scale. Ministers are betting that changes to what children are served each day will have more impact than repeated advice to families alone.

The proposal follows years of concern that school menus have not kept pace with public health guidance. If it is enforced properly, it could mark a more ambitious break from the old standards that have governed school food for years.

The challenge will be practical as much as political. Schools will need enough time, money and kitchen capacity to adjust menus without simply shifting the burden elsewhere. What sounds straightforward in policy terms can be difficult in the dining hall.

stories.title: Fried nuggets and steamed sponges off menu in school food overhaul in England
stories.summary: England’s first major update to school food standards in 13 years will ban items such as fried nuggets and steamed sponges from September.
stories.source_signal_title: Fried nuggets and steamed sponges off menu in school food overhaul in England
body: England is set for its first major update to school food standards in 13 years, with items such as fried nuggets and steamed sponges due to be removed from menus from September. The changes are part of a wider effort to reduce childhood obesity and improve the nutritional quality of school meals.

The scale of the problem helps explain the government’s intervention: NHS data for 2024 showed that 24% of nursery and primary school children were overweight or living with obesity. Ministers are betting that school menus can help shift those numbers over time.

The overhaul will be welcomed by campaigners who have argued that the standards had become outdated. But it will also put pressure on schools and caterers to deliver meals that are both healthier and still affordable and appealing to children.
The policy is a reminder that public health measures often work slowly and unevenly. The real test will be whether the new rules change what children actually eat, not just what appears on paper.

stories.title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
stories.summary: OpenAI and its rivals are trying to shape the public debate on AI through policy papers and thinktanks, but growing concern over jobs, cyber risks and trust may be harder to manage.
stories.source_signal_title: AI companies know they have an image problem. Will funding policy papers and thinktanks dig them out?
body: OpenAI and other major AI companies are trying to reshape the public debate around artificial intelligence through policy papers, thinktanks and Washington outreach. The effort is a sign that the industry recognises it has an image problem as concern grows over jobs, cyber risks and trust.

The timing is telling. Rather than focusing only on product launches or new data centres, some of the biggest firms are now trying to influence the policy conversation itself. OpenAI’s recent paper on an “industrial policy for the intelligence age” is one example of a broader attempt to present the technology as socially beneficial and politically manageable.

But a communications campaign can only go so far when public anxiety is rising. Polling and wider scepticism suggest that worries about job displacement, misinformation and security are not going away simply because companies fund sympathetic research or open more policy-friendly offices.

The larger question is whether these efforts are about genuine engagement or reputation management. In practice, they are likely to be both. The industry wants rules it can live with, but it also wants a better story to tell about why its power should be trusted.

stories.title: US summons bank bosses over cyber risks from Anthropic’s latest AI model
stories.summary: US officials have summoned major bank chiefs in Washington after Anthropic’s Claude Mythos model prompted fresh concern about cyber security risk.
stories.source_signal_title: US summons bank bosses over cyber risks from Anthropic’s latest AI model
body: US officials have summoned major bank chiefs to Washington after concerns emerged over the cyber risks posed by Anthropic’s latest AI model, Claude Mythos. The meeting, which reportedly included Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell, shows how quickly regulators are moving to assess the financial-sector implications of more capable AI systems.

The worry is straightforward: if a model can be used to automate or enhance cyber attacks, banks become a natural target. That makes the issue less about abstract technological progress and more about the resilience of a system that underpins payments, savings and credit.

The intervention also underlines how nervous policymakers have become about AI moving from productivity tool to security risk. For the banking sector, this is a warning that generative AI is now being treated not merely as a business opportunity but as a potential systemic threat.

What happens next will depend on how serious the vulnerabilities are judged to be and whether firms can prove their defences are keeping up. But the fact that the Treasury felt compelled to summon bank chiefs suggests official concern is already high.

stories.title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
stories.summary: Meta has removed ads recruiting people for social-media addiction lawsuits after losing a landmark trial in California over the effects of Facebook and related products.
stories.source_signal_title: Meta pulls Facebook ads recruiting for social media addiction lawsuits
body: Meta has removed Facebook ads recruiting people for social media addiction lawsuits after losing a landmark trial in California over the effects of Facebook and related products. The move suggests the company is trying to limit the spread of a legal campaign that could expose it to further claims about harm to users.

The case matters because it goes to the heart of the debate over how social media platforms affect behaviour, especially among younger users. A trial loss does not settle the science, but it does strengthen the hand of plaintiffs who argue that Meta knew more about the risks than it acknowledged.

By pulling the ads, Meta may be trying to reduce the visibility of litigation that could attract more claimants. But that does not remove the underlying dispute, which is likely to continue in courtrooms and in public debate about regulation.

The broader issue is whether social-media firms can keep presenting themselves as neutral platforms when they are under pressure over the effects of their products. The legal and reputational stakes are likely to remain high.

stories.title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
stories.summary: Breanna Olson has returned to the stage through a digital avatar, using brainwave technology to restore something of the expression and connection lost to motor neurone disease.
stories.source_signal_title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
body: Breanna Olson has returned to the stage through a digital avatar after motor neurone disease took away much of the physical expression her work depended on. The performance is a striking example of how brainwave technology is beginning to offer new forms of artistic participation for people living with severe disability.

The story matters because it is not simply about novelty. For performers whose bodies have changed dramatically, technology can restore something of the connection between intention and audience. In Olson’s case, the avatar allowed her to reclaim part of the presence that illness had interrupted.

That does not mean the technology replaces what was lost. Rather, it creates a different kind of stagecraft, one that can widen access while also raising questions about the future of live performance and the relationship between body, identity and art.

For now, the significance lies in what the project makes possible. It shows how assistive technology can be more than a tool of convenience; it can become a means of artistic continuity and emotional expression.

stories.title: Rory McIlroy holds nerve to be the Master again as rivals succumb to tension
stories.summary: Rory McIlroy defended the Masters at Augusta National, becoming only the fourth golfer to retain the title and matching Nick Faldo’s haul of six major championships.
stories.source_signal_title: Rory McIlroy holds nerve to be the Master again as rivals succumb to tension
body: Rory McIlroy has defended his Masters title at Augusta National, becoming only the fourth golfer to retain the green jacket and matching Nick Faldo’s tally of six major championships. The victory confirms his place among the game’s greats and adds another layer to a career that once seemed defined by missed chances at Augusta.

This was a tournament in which McIlroy was not at his best for long stretches, but he still found enough to finish on 12 under and win by one shot from Scottie Scheffler. That matters because it shows how elite golf is often decided not by brilliance alone but by the ability to hold nerve when others tighten up.

The result also deepens the historical comparison. Few players have managed to defend the Masters, and McIlroy now joins a small group that includes Jack Nicklaus, Faldo and Tiger Woods. For a player who had spent years chasing an Augusta breakthrough, this is no longer redemption alone but sustained excellence.
The question now is how far he can climb in the sport’s all-time rankings. With six major titles, he has already moved beyond Seve Ballesteros, and the record books are now the clear next target.

stories.title: Where does McIlroy rank among greats after Masters defence?
stories.summary: After winning back-to-back Masters titles, Rory McIlroy has strengthened his place among golf’s all-time greats and is now chasing the record books further.
stories.source_signal_title: Where does McIlroy rank among greats after Masters defence?
body: Rory McIlroy’s back-to-back Masters titles have strengthened his standing among golf’s all-time greats. Retaining the green jacket is rare enough; doing it after completing the career Grand Slam pushes him further into a very small historical club.

The significance lies not only in the win itself but in what it says about longevity at the top of the sport. Major titles are usually the best measure of greatness in golf, and McIlroy’s sixth has moved him further up an already crowded list of legends.

What he still lacks, compared with the absolute summit of the game, is a body of major wins on the level of Nicklaus or Woods. But the balance of his career now looks very different from a few years ago, when Augusta was more often a source of frustration than triumph.
The next chapter is straightforward: he is chasing more majors. The Masters defence has not answered every ranking question, but it has made the argument for McIlroy’s greatness much stronger.

stories.title: Cardiff stages thriller while Women’s Six Nations favourites show strength
stories.summary: Wales and Scotland produced a dramatic Women’s Six Nations opener in Cardiff, where 10,569 watched Scotland’s late comeback win at the Principality Stadium.
stories.source_signal_title: Cardiff stages thriller while Women’s Six Nations favourites show strength
body: Wales and Scotland produced a dramatic Women’s Six Nations opener in Cardiff, with 10,569 supporters at the Principality Stadium watching Scotland complete a late comeback. It was the kind of match that showed both the quality of the tournament and the continued challenge of giving women’s rugby the crowd it deserves.

The finish was the most striking part of the day. Wales appeared to have done enough, only for Scotland to claw their way back in the closing minutes and steal the win. That kind of late swing can define a championship, especially in a competition where momentum matters as much as points.

Elsewhere, the favourites also underlined their strength, which may mean the title race quickly narrows around the sides expected to contend. But Cardiff’s thriller offered something more immediate: proof that the women’s game can produce the same tension and drama that have long made the men’s tournament so compelling.

The attendance also points to a wider issue. The crowd was a record for a Wales-Scotland fixture in Wales, yet the atmosphere might have been better served by a more compact venue. The rugby was worthy of a bigger stage, even if the numbers still lag behind the quality on show.

stories.title: Man City have psychological edge over Arsenal - Rooney
stories.summary: Wayne Rooney says Manchester City now hold the psychological edge over Arsenal in the Premier League title race after their latest win over Chelsea.
stories.source_signal_title: Man City have psychological edge over Arsenal - Rooney
body: Wayne Rooney believes Manchester City now hold the psychological edge over Arsenal in the Premier League title race. That view follows City’s latest win over Chelsea, which has increased the pressure on the leaders as the season enters its decisive stretch.

The idea of a psychological edge matters in a title race because points are only part of the story. When rivals keep winning and the frontrunner starts to slip, confidence can shift quickly, and the burden of expectation can become as important as form.

City’s recent surge has made Arsenal’s position more precarious. With every good result, Pep Guardiola’s side can present themselves as the team with momentum, experience and the habit of handling pressure at the right time.
Rooney’s point is not that the title is decided, but that the balance of belief may already be changing. In a close contest, that can be decisive long before the maths is settled.
